messages.content: In angular application, I have one module named A, which has routing module in which they specified component A to load when path is empty. I have imported module A to my module B to use one of exported component from module A. In MY Module B I also specified my component B to load when path is empty. The issue is When I navigate to my module B router path, component A of module A is rendered instead module B component B

I think I give some more information regarding this issue, Module A is imported as regular method while module B is imported as lazy loading, I want to fix this without touching module A because it from other project. While I navigate to module B router path, I lazy load it

Reason why using for child, This is not main module of my angular application, essentially my angular application is separated into many projects and it is one the project, here  path: RouteDirection.medicalHistory, is my module B where I am working on which is lazy loaded, I will provide you app module imports
 imports: [

    ),

where communicationModule in module A

Given your configuration for Module B (MedicalHistoryHomeModule), which is lazy-loaded, and Module A (CommunicationModule), which is eagerly loaded and specified directly in the app module's imports, the conflict seems to be due to having two different components mapped to the empty path ('').

When Angular initializes the router, it merges the route configurations from all loaded modules, starting with eager-loaded modules, followed by lazy-loaded modules when they are loaded. If you have two routes with the same path (''), the router will match the first one it encounters, which could explain why Module A's `CommunicationComponent` is rendered instead of Module B's `MedicalHistoryContainerComponent`.

To resolve this conflict, you have a few options:

1. **Differentiate the base paths**: Ensure that Module A (CommunicationModule) and Module B (MedicalHistoryHomeModule) have different non-empty base paths in your main routing configuration so that they do not both try to use the empty path.

2. **Use lazy-loading for both**: If possible, move the import of Module A (CommunicationModule) to be lazy-loaded as well, similar to how Module B is set up. This will help ensure that the empty path in Module A does not take precedence over the one in Module B because it will only be loaded when necessary.

3. **Refactor CommunicationModule's routes**: If you cannot touch Module A (as you mentioned), you can still refactor the route for Module A in the AppRoutingModule of the project that imports Module A to give it a non-empty base path.

4. **Ensure correct ordering**: Since lazy-loaded modules are loaded asynchronously, their routes are not known at the time the application starts. This means that any eager-loaded module's routes will take precedence. To ensure the correct component is loaded, you may reverse the order in which the routes are checked. This can often be managed by the structure of your routing configuration or by using a route guard to conditionally redirect to the appropriate component.

5. **Review route guards**: Ensure that the `OktaAuthJsGuard` and other route guards are not causing unintentional behavior with the routing logic. Also, check if there's any route resolution or redirection logic that could be directing the user to Module A's route instead of Module B's.

Since you mentioned that you cannot modify Module A, you will need to ensure that the empty path ('') routes in Module B have a higher priority. This is achieved by having the lazy-loaded route configuration take precedence by not conflicting with the eager-loaded routes.

If you continue to encounter issues, you might consider consulting the Angular Router documentation or seeking assistance from other developers who are familiar with your specific project structure and can offer more targeted advice.

Refactor CommunicationModule’s routes: If you cannot touch Module A (as you mentioned), you can still refactor the route for Module A in the AppRoutingModule of the project that imports Module A to give it a non-empty base path.
how to do it
The problem is module A also imported lazily when navigate to it's route URL, since I want to use one of the components from module B, it is eager loaded in my project app module
Sorry I am not using component A of module A in module B but in app component itself, and component A is exported correctly in module a
Main point is module A is imported both ways, eager and lazy loaded when navigate to it route path
he goal is to avoid having an empty path '' in the lazy-loaded configuration of Module A. But I cannot change it,
The problem is not with lazy loaded module A, it is with eager loaded module A, which I need to import to use of its exported component in my app component
Can you repeat my problem exactly and offer your solution
